Description: Typical campus food operation without major disruption
Expected Consequences: Stable demand, normal production, ordinary waste and queue patterns
Recovery Objective: Maintain baseline mission achievement and EVI
Primary Metrics: EVI, satisfaction, profit, waste reduction, fulfillment
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Use as reference scenario for calibration and model comparison
Campus Food Example: Regular teaching week with predictable lunch demand

Description: Sudden increase in student meal demand during lunch period
Expected Consequences: Longer queues, higher shortage risk, higher vendor utilization
Recovery Objective: Fulfill additional demand without excessive waste or waiting time
Primary Metrics: Fulfillment, waiting time, satisfaction, vendor utilization, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Increase vendor mission intensity, redirect students, synchronize supply and delivery
Campus Food Example: Unexpected campus event creates extra lunch demand

Description: Sustained increase in food demand due to exam schedules and longer campus stay
Expected Consequences: Higher meal demand, stronger peak loads, risk of shortage and student dissatisfaction
Recovery Objective: Maintain affordability, nutrition, and short waiting time during peak academic stress
Primary Metrics: Mission achievement, satisfaction, nutrition, shortage, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Pre-plan production, advance supplier delivery, issue student routing recommendations
Campus Food Example: Students remain longer on campus and need more meals during exams

Description: Reduced student presence lowers food demand
Expected Consequences: Overproduction risk, low vendor utilization, lower supplier turnover
Recovery Objective: Reduce waste while preserving minimum vendor viability
Primary Metrics: Waste reduction, profit, utilization, vendor viability
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Recommend lower production, reduced delivery schedule, temporary menu adjustment
Campus Food Example: Campus population drops after final exams

Description: Ingredient and operating costs increase while student budgets remain limited
Expected Consequences: Reduced affordability, lower vendor margin, pressure to increase prices
Recovery Objective: Maintain affordability and vendor viability
Primary Metrics: Affordability, profit, satisfaction, mission achievement, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Recommend subsidy, menu substitution, supplier renegotiation, cost-sensitive routing
Campus Food Example: Rice, oil, and meat prices increase significantly

Description: Ingredient supply becomes limited or delayed
Expected Consequences: Ingredient shortages, reduced menu availability, lower meal production
Recovery Objective: Avoid critical stockout and maintain essential meal availability
Primary Metrics: Shortage, fulfillment, nutrition, supplier reliability, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Recommend menu substitution, supplier reallocation, production prioritization
Campus Food Example: Supplier cannot deliver vegetables due to external disruption

Description: One or more vendors cannot operate due to illness, equipment failure, or closure
Expected Consequences: Capacity shortage, longer queues, demand redistribution, lower menu diversity
Recovery Objective: Redistribute demand and preserve minimum service level
Primary Metrics: Fulfillment, waiting time, satisfaction, resilience, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Redirect students, increase production at remaining vendors, activate backup vendors
Campus Food Example: Popular vendor closes unexpectedly during lunch period

Description: Kitchen or payment equipment failure reduces vendor throughput
Expected Consequences: Queue formation, slower service, lower production, possible wasted ingredients
Recovery Objective: Minimize queue and reroute demand to functioning capacity
Primary Metrics: Waiting time, shortage, utilization, satisfaction
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Issue rerouting recommendations and adjust production plans of other vendors
Campus Food Example: Rice cooker or payment terminal fails at Vendor_A

Description: Digital services or food production infrastructure are temporarily unavailable
Expected Consequences: Online ordering disruption, payment delay, lower recommendation effectiveness
Recovery Objective: Maintain fallback operations and essential food service
Primary Metrics: Service continuity, resilience, satisfaction, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Switch to fallback policy, reduce dependence on online data, prioritize essential operations
Campus Food Example: Campus internet outage disables online ordering dashboard

Description: Health protocols reduce physical capacity and change demand patterns
Expected Consequences: Reduced seating, higher delivery dependence, compliance burden, changed demand timing
Recovery Objective: Maintain safe food access and compliance
Primary Metrics: Safety compliance, delivery reliability, satisfaction, mission achievement
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Increase delivery recommendations, stagger demand, enforce compliance constraints
Campus Food Example: Physical distancing reduces canteen capacity

Description: Food safety issue reduces trust and triggers regulatory intervention
Disturbance Magnitude: Affected vendor demand -40% to -90%; inspection intensity +50%
Expected Consequences: Demand shift, reputational damage, compliance cost, vendor suspension risk
Recovery Objective: Restore safety, trust, and service continuity
Primary Metrics: Trust score, compliance rate, satisfaction, vendor viability, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Redirect demand, trigger inspection, recommend corrective action, monitor trust recovery
Campus Food Example: A vendor receives repeated hygiene complaints

Description: A new vendor enters the ecosystem, increasing capacity and menu diversity
Expected Consequences: More competition, changed demand distribution, possible lower queues
Recovery Objective: Integrate new vendor while preserving fairness and viability
Primary Metrics: Demand distribution, vendor profit, satisfaction, menu diversity
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Rebalance student recommendations and supplier allocation
Campus Food Example: Healthy-food vendor opens on campus

Description: A vendor leaves the ecosystem permanently or temporarily
Expected Consequences: Lower diversity, reduced capacity, demand redistribution, supplier contract adjustment
Recovery Objective: Maintain service level and prevent overload of remaining vendors
Primary Metrics: Fulfillment, queue time, vendor utilization, satisfaction
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Redistribute demand, identify replacement vendor, adjust supplier plans
Campus Food Example: Vendor_B closes at end of semester

Description: University introduces minimum nutrition or healthy-menu requirements
Expected Consequences: Improved nutrition but possible cost increase and menu adaptation burden
Recovery Objective: Improve nutrition without reducing affordability or vendor viability
Primary Metrics: Nutrition score, affordability, profit, satisfaction, mission achievement
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Recommend healthy menu mix, subsidy, supplier adjustment
Campus Food Example: University requires healthier meal options

Description: Single-use plastic packaging is restricted or banned
Disturbance Magnitude: Packaging cost +5% to +25%; plastic waste -50% to -90%
Expected Consequences: Lower plastic waste, higher packaging cost, operational adjustment
Recovery Objective: Reduce environmental impact while preserving service efficiency
Primary Metrics: Sustainability, waste reduction, affordability, vendor profit
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Recommend reusable packaging, menu packaging redesign, subsidy if needed
Campus Food Example: Campus prohibits single-use plastic containers

Description: Carbon-related costs are applied to logistics, energy use, or food sourcing
Expected Consequences: Higher operating cost, incentive for low-emission routing and sourcing
Recovery Objective: Reduce carbon impact while maintaining affordability and service reliability
Primary Metrics: CO2 proxy, sustainability, affordability, profit, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Recommend low-carbon suppliers, route optimization, menu substitution
Campus Food Example: Delivery and sourcing routes are penalized for high emissions

Description: University expands meal support for low-income students
Expected Consequences: Improved affordability and welfare, increased demand, subsidy budget pressure
Recovery Objective: Improve equity while maintaining budget and vendor viability
Primary Metrics: Affordability, equity proxy, mission achievement, budget use, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Recommend targeted subsidies and production planning
Campus Food Example: University provides meal vouchers to scholarship students

Description: Students shift preference toward healthier, cheaper, faster, or trending meals
Expected Consequences: Unbalanced vendor utilization, menu mismatch, potential waste in unpopular items
Recovery Objective: Adapt menu and production to new demand patterns
Primary Metrics: Demand forecast accuracy, waste, satisfaction, vendor utilization
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Update menu recommendation, vendor production, and supplier allocation
Campus Food Example: Students suddenly prefer healthy bowls over fried meals

Description: Heavy rain or extreme heat changes mobility, demand, delivery, and supply reliability
Disturbance Magnitude: On-site demand -10% to -40%; delivery demand +20% to +80%; logistics capacity -10% to -30%
Expected Consequences: More delivery requests, supply delay, lower walk-in demand, route congestion
Recovery Objective: Maintain access and reduce delay during environmental disruption
Primary Metrics: Delivery reliability, satisfaction, fulfillment, resilience
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Shift to delivery-aware recommendations and route planning
Campus Food Example: Heavy rain reduces canteen visits but increases food delivery demand

Description: Improved forecasting model is introduced into the operator layer
Expected Consequences: Better production planning, reduced waste and shortage, higher trust in MSRS
Recovery Objective: Improve recommendation accuracy and stakeholder acceptance
Primary Metrics: Prediction accuracy, waste reduction, acceptance rate, EVI
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Use improved forecast in PUDAL Understanding stage
Campus Food Example: AI model predicts lunch demand more accurately from calendar and weather data

Description: Demand surge, cost increase, supply reduction, and capacity loss occur together
Expected Consequences: High shortage risk, affordability stress, vendor margin pressure, coordination burden
Recovery Objective: Preserve essential service, mission achievement, and resilience under systemic stress
Primary Metrics: Robustness index, EVI, resilience, mission achievement, shortage, affordability
MSRS / PUDAL Response: Activate balanced-EVI policy, prioritize essential meals, recommend subsidy, synchronize production and supply
Campus Food Example: Exam week coincides with inflation, supplier delay, and vendor equipment failure
